ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for yakitori bibimbap fusion bowl; mapped to closest parent class 'mixed rice dish' (FOODON_00002809).
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible match for a mixed rice bowl with grilled meat and vegetables; no exact code exists for yakitori bibimbap fusion.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this fusion dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl weighing approximately 550 g, containing Japanese rice, charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori, fried garlic, chili garlic flakes, seasoned bean sprouts, spinach, kimchi, gochujang, and a half-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods composite estimate for mixed rice bowl with grilled chicken, vegetables, egg, and fermented condiments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for this Japanese-Korean fusion dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain rice, grilled chicken thigh, fried garlic, hard-boiled egg, cooked spinach, bean sprouts, kimchi, and gochujang. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. Values for monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the composite estimate is insufficiently precise.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Japanese short-grain white rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this fusion dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The protein (chicken, egg), fat (sesame oil, fried garlic), fibre (spinach, bean sprouts, kimchi), and fermented components (kimchi, gochujang) are expected to moderately attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response in practice. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 79.9) / 100 ≈ 57.5, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 14.55 × 5.5 = 80.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg per 100 g; estimated 1800 mg per 550 g serving from soy sauce, gochujang, and kimchi
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and gochujang, ask for kimchi served on the side, and consider low-sodium soy sauce alternatives, as a full bowl may contribute approximately 75–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI Japanese short-grain rice (GI ~72) with estimated glycemic load of ~57.5 per full bowl; partially offset by protein, fat, fibre, and fermented components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced rice portion and lighter gochujang application to lower the glycaemic load, while the protein from chicken and egg, dietary fibre from vegetables, and fermented kimchi components help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (327 mg/100 g) and moderate cholesterol (45 mg/100 g); low saturated fat (1.45 g/100 g) and presence of anti-inflammatory garlic and sesame components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage sodium and cholesterol intake, noting that the low saturated fat content, anti-inflammatory polyphenols from garlic and sesame oil, and potassium-containing spinach and bean sprouts partially offset cardiovascular risk, but the high sodium load from condiments remains the primary concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components (kimchi probiotics, garlic polyphenols, spinach antioxidants) with low saturated fat; caution for capsaicin-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the probiotic and anti-inflammatory components of kimchi, garlic, and spinach, but should monitor symptom response to chili and gochujang, and request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Fried Garlic and Chili Garlic Flakes Chicken Yakitori Bibimbap Bowl is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish sold in Singapore through food delivery platforms. It features charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori skewers placed over a base of Japanese short-grain white rice, topped with crispy fried garlic pieces and chili garlic flakes for aromatic depth and heat. The bowl is accompanied by seasoned bean sprouts, blanched spinach, kimchi, a spoonful of gochujang (Korean fermented chili paste), and a half-boiled egg. The preparation combines Japanese yakitori grilling technique with Korean bibimbap assembly, creating a dish that is simultaneously smoky, savoury, spicy, and umami-rich. The texture profile includes tender grilled chicken, chewy short-grain rice, crunchy fried garlic, crisp bean sprouts, and a soft runny egg yolk. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 136 kcal per 100 g with 8.2 g protein, 5.1 g fat, and 14.6 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce, gochujang, and kimchi, making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycaemic index is estimated at 72 based on the dominant Japanese short-grain rice component, placing it in the high-GI category, though the protein, fat, fibre, and fermented ingredients moderate the postprandial glucose response. Saturated fat is low at 1.45 g per 100 g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken and egg content, and contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce. It is culturally positioned as a fusion comfort food popular in Singapore's diverse food delivery market.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bibimbap bowl?
Is this dish high in sodium?
Can I eat bibimbap if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of bibimbap?
How much protein is in a chicken bibimbap bowl?
What allergens are in yakitori bibimbap?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this Japanese-Korean fusion dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central composite of component ingredients (grilled chicken, short-grain rice, egg, vegetables, kimchi, gochujang, fried garlic) and cross-checked against the ai_generated input values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant Japanese short-grain rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is set to medium reflecting the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fusion dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Foundation Foods as of 2024; individual component entries do not provide sufficient precision for a reliable composite fatty acid estimate. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the full composite dish (including kimchi, gochujang, and fried garlic) is not available with sufficient precision in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this multi-component fusion dish are not available with sufficient precision in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g per 100 g is a low but non-zero value consistent with minor naturally occurring trans fats from the grilled chicken component. 5. The dish is a Singapore food delivery item (GrabFood) with Japanese-Korean fusion identity; primary cuisine classification is Japanese-Korean Fusion with country of origin Singapore. 6. Ethnic tags from input ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry') were not carried forward as they are inconsistent with the actual dish composition. 7. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; actual dish GI may be lower due to protein, fat, fibre, and fermented ingredient attenuation. 8. Halal status is unconfirmed as the source is a food delivery platform without certified halal documentation; is_halal set to false pending verification.
